summary:
• Independence Day Celebrations: Chattanooga is gearing up for America's 250th Independence Day with events like Pops on the River at Coolidge Park and the United 250 finale on the riverfront. Ensure public safety and traffic management plans are in place.
  • Environmental Initiative: A successful cleanup at Citico Creek resulted in the removal of approximately 1,000 lbs of litter, showcasing effective community partnership with Tennessee Valley Federal Credit Union and News Channel 9.
  • Cultural Development: Rebecca Brantley has been appointed as curator and director of the Institute of Contemporary Art at UTC, enhancing Chattanooga's cultural landscape and academic offerings in the arts.

items.title: 🎆 Please take a few minutes to review the Collegedale Police Department’s 2026 Freedom Festival Parking & Post-Event Tr...
items.content_text:
🎆 Please take a few minutes to review the Collegedale Police Department’s 2026 Freedom Festival Parking & Post-Event Traffic Plan before heading to this year’s celebration, this Saturday, July 4th.
 🚗 Where you park will determine which direction you are sent after the fireworks, so planning ahead will help everyone get home safely and more efficiently.
 🇺🇸 We look forward to celebrating America’s 250th birthday with you at Collegedale’s Freedom Festival!
🚨🚧PLEASE SHARE‼️🚧🚨
 🚗🚓 2026 Freedom Festival Parking & Traffic Plan 🚗🚓
 Collegedale’s Freedom Festival, on Saturday, July 4th, is almost here & this year we’re celebrating America’s 250th birthday with our annual fireworks celebration.
 Before you arrive, please take a few minutes to review the parking and post-event traffic plans. Where you choose to park will determine which direction you’ll be sent after the fireworks are over, so preplanning now will make getting home much easier later.
 Please park only in approved parking areas. American Tow & Recovery will be on site and will be towing vehicles that are improperly parked, blocking access, or obstructing traffic.
 Come early, park smart, enjoy the celebration, and help us keep traffic moving safely for everyone.
 We’ll see you at the Freedom Festival!

items.title: This wonderful group of Tennessee Valley Federal Credit Union volunteers helped us clean up ~1,000lbs of litter from Cit...
items.content_text:
This wonderful group of Tennessee Valley Federal Credit Union volunteers helped us clean up ~1,000lbs of litter from Citico Creek!
TVFCU has partnered with News Channel 9 and the City of Chattanooga in the Clean Currents initiative. 🌳🌊
 This week a group of out employees spent time at the Citico Creek cleaning up trash from the area's waters. This creek directly feeds into the Tennessee River. By taking the time to pick up trash in and around Citico Creek, we are helping to keep the trash from flowing into the river. Thank you to the News Channel 9 crew and the City of Chattanooga for coordinating and allowing us to be a part of this effort!

items.title: CARTA Holiday Service Schedule – Independence Day Weekend  In observance of Independence Day, CARTA will operate regular...
items.content_text:
CARTA Holiday Service Schedule – Independence Day Weekend 
 In observance of Independence Day, CARTA will operate regular weekday service on Friday, July 3 and Monday, July 6. The administrative office will also remain open on both days.
 Saturday, July 4
 The following services will not operate:
 - Fixed Route Service
 - Care-A-Van
 - CARTA GO
 - North Shore Shuttle
 The following services will operate on a Sunday schedule:
 - Downtown Shuttle
 - Northshore Express (34)
 - St. Elmo/Incline Shuttle (15)
 Sunday, July 5
 All bus services will operate on their regular Sunday schedules.
 Please plan your trips accordingly and have a safe and happy Independence Day weekend! 🇺🇸🚌

items.title: Art historian and curator Rebecca Brantley has joined UTC as curator and director of the Institute of Contemporary Art (...
items.content_text:
Art historian and curator Rebecca Brantley has joined UTC as curator and director of the Institute of Contemporary Art (ICA Chattanooga) and a faculty associate in the Department of Art. 🎨 
 Brantley comes to UTC from Piedmont University in Demorest, Georgia, where, as director of the Mason-Scharfenstein Museum of Art and an associate professor, she developed an exhibition program centered on the American South, expanded the museum’s audience and collection, and helped lead a 58% increase in attendance in recent years.
 🔗  Read more here: https://blog.utc.edu/…/rebecca-brantley-to-lead-utc-instit…/

items.title: This Fourth of July, celebrate with confidence and don’t lose your independence. If you choose to drink, do not drive. T...
items.content_text:
This Fourth of July, celebrate with confidence and don’t lose your independence. If you choose to drink, do not drive.
 The National Highway Traffic Safety Administration (NHTSA) reminds us that even a small amount of alcohol can impair judgment and reaction time. 
 Plan ahead & make the smart choice by securing a sober ride, using a rideshare service, or arranging your safe transportation before the festivities start.
 Enjoy the fireworks, delicious food, and the celebration, and ensure everyone gets home safely. You’ve got this!
 For more information, visit https://www.nhtsa.gov/celebrate-america-safely-july-4th.

items.title: Saturday, July 11th, join HCEMS, Blood Assurance, and our public safety partners at the Scenic City Saves Lives Communit...
items.content_text:
Saturday, July 11th, join HCEMS, Blood Assurance, and our public safety partners at the Scenic City Saves Lives Community Blood Drive at Miller Park from 9:00 a.m. to 2:00 p.m.
 Your donation helps ensure lifesaving whole blood is available when seconds matter most. One donation can save up to three lives.
 We are challenging every first responder agency in Hamilton County—fire, law enforcement, EMS, dispatch, emergency management, and beyond to sign up, show up and donate. Then challenge another agency to do the same. Let's see which department brings the biggest team and makes the biggest impact!
 Let's gooooo, Chattanooga! Together, we can prove that when our community needs us, we always answer the call.
 Click the link below to sign up today!!!
 www.bloodassurance.org/scenic

items.title: Don’t wait - hydrate!! 💧 We’re facing a huge heat wave Chattanooga, it’s important to follow these safe practices!
items.content_text:
Don’t wait - hydrate!! 💧
 We’re facing a huge heat wave Chattanooga, it’s important to follow these safe practices!
 - Drink plenty of water
 - Limit strenuous outdoor activity during the hottest part of the day
 - Stay cool indoors when possible
 - Check on family, friends, and neighbors
 (Children under 4 & Adults over 65 are at greater risk!)
 - Never leave children or pets in a vehicle

items.title: 💖 July Donation Drive: Support Love, Period. of Chattanooga! 💖 This July, help us support Love, Period., a Chattanooga...
items.content_text:
💖 July Donation Drive: Support Love, Period. of Chattanooga! 💖
 This July, help us support Love, Period., a Chattanooga-based nonprofit with a simple but powerful mission:
 "We are showing up with care, compassion, and products—period."
 Founded in January 2024 at St. Timothy’s Episcopal Church on Signal Mountain, Love, Period. has grown through partnerships with local Episcopal churches, including St. Peter’s Episcopal Church on Ashland Terrace. Together, they are helping provide essential menstrual products to our community through local schools, the Northside Neighborhood House, and the Red Bank Food Pantry.
 🩷 How You Can Help Donate menstrual hygiene products throughout the month of July.
 📍 Drop-Off Location • Red Bank City Hall
 🕣 Donation Hours Monday–Friday | 8:30 AM–4:30 PM
 Every donation helps ensure that people in our community have access to basic necessities with dignity and support.
 #LovePeriod #RedBankTN #CommunityCare #SupportLocal #PeriodPovertyAwareness #GiveBack #RedBankCommunityCenter #LovePeriodChattanooga #StPetersEpiscopalChurch

items.title: Article by DomesticShelters.org  Leaving an abusive relationship doesn’t always mean the fight is over.  For many surviv...
items.content_text:
Article by DomesticShelters.org 
 Leaving an abusive relationship doesn’t always mean the fight is over. 
 For many survivors, the courtroom becomes the next place control shows up—through custody battles, protection orders, and drawn-out legal tactics. That’s why having a trauma-informed attorney who truly understands coercive control and post-separation abuse can make a huge difference.
 And if cost feels impossible, you’re not alone. Financial abuse often leaves survivors starting from zero, but options like legal aid, referrals, limited-scope help (paying for only part of a case), and survivor-informed guidance can open doors. 
 If you or someone you care about is facing this, what’s one question you wish every survivor could ask before hiring a lawyer? 
  🔗 https://lnkd.in/giufC6PH
 #EndDomesticViolence #CoerciveControl #TraumaInformedLawyer #LegalAidForSurvivors #KnowYourRights

items.title: France has demonstrated resilience amid recent global shocks, but challenges remain to secure stronger and more sustaina...
items.content_text:
France has demonstrated resilience amid recent global shocks, but challenges remain to secure stronger and more sustainable growth.
 Public finances are under pressure: the public debt ratio has nearly doubled in 25 years and borrowing costs are among the highest in the euro area. Ensuring fiscal sustainability is a top priority.
 Necessary reforms include:
 ➡️ Improve public spending efficiency
 ➡️ Resume the implementation of the 2023 pension reform
 ➡️ Make taxation fairer and more job-friendly
 Yesterday, #OECD Secretary-General Mathias Cormann and the Minister of Economy, Finance, and Industrial, Energy and Digital Sovereignty for France, Roland Lescure, launched the Economic Survey of France.
 Learn more: https://brnw.ch/21x3NsL

items.title: Video in the comments! Check out this student-created step routine from our Spring Break 2026 STEM From Dance program. T...
items.content_text:
Video in the comments!
 Check out this student-created step routine from our Spring Break 2026 STEM From Dance program.
 Thanks to support from the Arts Build Community Cultural Connections (CCC) Grant, girls from the Boys & Girls Clubs of Chattanooga's Highland Park and East Lake Clubs spent their spring break exploring the connection between STEM, dance, and environmental responsibility.
 Throughout the week, students learned about carbon footprints and how our everyday choices affect the Earth. One student shared:
 "High carbon activities are driving, leaving lights on when you're not using them at home, and using a lot of energy."
 The girls brought their learning to life by creating this original step routine inspired by what they discovered. At the end of the week, they completed quizzes to reflect on what they had learned about sustainability and environmental responsibility.
 Thank you to ArtsBuild for making this program possible through the Community Cultural Connections (CCC) Grant, and to Barking Legs Theater for generously providing space for us throughout the week. Your support helped create an inspiring environment where these young leaders could learn, create, and grow through both STEM and the arts.

items.title: All Chattanooga Fire stations are drop-off locations for the Forgotten Child Fund’s first annual “Loper Christmas in Jul...
items.content_text:
All Chattanooga Fire stations are drop-off locations for the Forgotten Child Fund’s first annual “Loper Christmas in July” Toy Drive.
 The FCF has partnered with KZ106’s Loper Show and community supporters throughout the Chattanooga area for this endeavor. 
 Throughout the entire month of July, residents are encouraged to donate new, unused, unwrapped toys to help ensure that local children and families in need are not forgotten during the holiday season.
 With the rising cost of groceries, housing, utilities, and everyday living expenses, more families than ever are struggling to provide even the basic necessities for their children.
 Together, the community can help make sure no child is forgotten this Christmas!

🏅Today marks a major milestone for students and workforce development: the new federal Workforce Pell Grant program is now live nationwide.

For the first time, eligible students can use Pell Grants to access short-term, high-quality workforce training programs that prepare them for high-skill, high-wage, and in-demand careers. This expansion creates new pathways to opportunity while helping employers meet workforce needs.

As states work to implement Workforce Pell, readiness varies across the country. This resource from Opportunity Data provides a helpful snapshot of where each state stands in issuing public guidance and accepting program applications: 
🔗 https://lnkd.in/eXM5YWtr 

Learn more about the Workforce Pell program from the U.S. Department of Education:
🔗https://lnkd.in/ej5QrRGc

#Chattanooga20 #HigherEducation #WorkforceDevelopment #WorkforcePell

☀️ July is Head Start Enrollment Month! ☀️
 Give your child a good start towards a bright future! 🎒✨ We will be holding Head Start Registration Days throughout the month of July to help families register for the next school year.
 Head Start offers high-quality early childhood education, healthy meals, and family support services at no cost to qualifying families.
 What to bring:
 🪪 Identification with photo of the father, mother or guardian
 👤 Proof of the child's age (Birth Certificate)
 💼 Proof of income (Tax return, W-2 or pay stubs)
 Spots fill up fast, so don't wait! ⏰

items.title: We loved working with Greg Kahn as he explored Chattanooga through his lens. We’re especially proud that he selected Coo...
items.content_text:
We loved working with Greg Kahn as he explored Chattanooga through his lens.
 We’re especially proud that he selected Coolidge Park and Renaissance Park as two of the featured locations for this incredible photo story, showcasing the beauty, energy, and sense of place found in our city’s public spaces.
 Thank you, Greg, for helping share Chattanooga with the world, one stunning image at a time. Be sure to check out the full story and see our parks through a National Geographic perspective!
Chattanooga, Tennessee, lives up to its Scenic City name. From Rock City’s panoramic overlooks and the awe‑inspiring Ruby Falls to the quiet trails of Reflection Riding and Glen Falls and the riverfront charm of downtown Coolidge Park, the city welcomes families with its blend of outdoor adventure and downtown attractions. See more of Nat Geo photographer Greg Kahn’s family trip here: https://on.natgeo.com/uk1yZj

🏆 Nominations and applications are now open for the 2026 Technology Excellence (TechX) Awards!

items.content_text:
🚨 Did you know 1 in 5 pets that go missing are from being frightened by loud noises? Although fireworks are illegal in the City of Charleston, it important you look after your furry companions over the holiday. 🐶 
 Now is a good time to update their chip information, ensure their tags are up to date and if you plan to have them outside with you, keep them on a leash!
 - Residents should report emergency incidents, such as fireworks resulting in fire or emergency, by calling 911.
 - Complaints regarding fireworks use or discharge that do not pose an immediate threat should be directed to the non-emergency numbers below:
 Charleston County Consolidated 9-1-1 Center: 843-743-7200
 Charleston Sheriff's Office Non-Emergency Line: 843-202-1700

items.content_text:
Can you imagine if we did this everywhere? Including our neighborhoods?
Thousands of flowers and hundreds of trees, shrubs, and native grasses now help beautify a stretch of US 27 in Chattanooga as part of a pilot project between TDOT, the City of Chattanooga - Government , and the Tennessee Interstate Conservancy. The first of its kind pilot project was announced back in 2024, but it takes time for the flowers, trees, and shrubs to be planted, take root, and flourish.  The beautification efforts stretch along US 27 from 6th street to Riverfront Parkway.

items.title: For two weeks in June, local high school students spent part of their summer break at UTC learning how the federal court...
items.content_text:
For two weeks in June, local high school students spent part of their summer break at UTC learning how the federal court system works. 👏 
 Twenty-five high schoolers participated in “The Supreme Court in My Hometown,” a national civics education program sponsored by the Supreme Court Historical Society, the U.S. Court of Appeals for the Sixth Circuit, the U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of Tennessee and the Adolph S. Ochs Professor of Government at UTC, Dr. Michelle D. Deardorff. 
 Participants heard presentations from judges, attorneys and professors, worked with UTC student mentors and visited the local courthouse, exploring the federal court system. 
 🔗  Read more here: https://blog.utc.edu/…/inside-the-judicial-branch-national…/

items.title: Congratulations to the first Early Childhood Neighborhood Council in Alton Park! Since December, this incredible group of parent,...
items.content_text:
Congratulations to the first Early Childhood Neighborhood Council in Alton Park!

Since December, this incredible group of parent, grandparent, and caregiver leaders has met to learn together, share their experiences, identify the strengths and needs of young children in Alton Park, and build the confidence and skills to advocate for families in their neighborhood.

This wasn't a parenting class. These resident leaders are helping shape how our community supports children before they ever enter kindergarten. Their ideas and lived experiences will help guide the work of more than 40 organizations that have committed to serving Alton Park more intentionally as we work toward becoming an Early Learning Community where every young child can thrive.

To this first Early Childhood Neighborhood Council: Thank you for your time, your honesty, your leadership, and your commitment to the youngest children in your neighborhood. The relationships you've built, the stories you've shared, and the leadership you've developed are already helping shape a stronger future for Alton Park's youngest children.

Special thanks to: the ELN Local Action Fund for investing in this pilot and helping make this work possible; to Jakita Blackmon for being the group's dedicated and trusted facilitator; to Erika Roberts (Velvet Poetry Productions) for supporting the Wisdom Lens project for the council; to Katherlyn L Geter for equipping the group with advocacy skills; and to the The Bethlehem Center for allowing us to host monthly meetings at the facility.

(Not pictured: LaTosha Tate, Brittany Strickland, and Jermiya Ballard.) 💙

 Throughout his career, he earned the respect of those around him through steady leadership, professionalism, and an unwavering commitment to the job. Whether mentoring younger firefighters or leading emergency responses, he set the standard for what it means to serve with integrity and compassion.
 His contributions to our department and community have left a lasting impact, and his leadership will be missed.
 Congratulations, Captain Stone, on your well-earned retirement! Thank you for your many years of service. We wish you all the best in the years ahead!

items.title: The OECD Survey on Drivers of Trust in Public Institutions 2026 Results is out now. Levels of trust in government have s...
items.content_text:
The OECD Survey on Drivers of Trust in Public Institutions 2026 Results is out now.
 Levels of trust in government have steadied since the survey began in 2021, with 40% of people expressing trust in their national government and signs of improvement in several countries. 
 68% of citizens believe voting works - an essential foundation of democracy - yet only 31% feel they have a real influence on what government does.
 This is one of several significant gaps revealed by the #OECDTrustSurvey, which explores the drivers of trust in public institutions and points to where reform is most needed.
 The analysis also shows that people trust the public services they count on every day, but are less confident about government’s ability to tackle long-term challenges.
 To protect and build trust, governments need to open up genuine opportunities to participate, increase transparency, ground decisions in evidence, and make sure new technology earns people’s confidence.
 The survey’s findings will be at the centre of discussions at the 2026 Global Forum on Building Trust and Reinforcing Democracy in October.
 Learn more about the Results: https://brnw.ch/21x3LLE

items.title: Official development assistance (ODA) for health could decline by up to 46% in 2026. A new #OECD policy brief projects t...
items.content_text:
Official development assistance (ODA) for health could decline by up to 46% in 2026.
 A new #OECD policy brief projects that health will face the deepest cuts among major development sectors, falling below pre-pandemic levels.
 ➡️ Population and reproductive health could see a decline of up to 54.1%
 ➡️ ODA for malaria control could fall by up to 59.6%
 ➡️ ODA for tuberculosis control could fall by up to 57.2%
 ➡️ ODA for other infectious-disease control could fall by up to 40.4%
 Health bears the deepest projected sectoral cuts, but it is not the only area affected. Humanitarian aid and support to government and civil society are also projected to decline significantly, by 40.3% and 39.8% respectively between 2024 and 2026.
 These reductions could affect funding for pandemic preparedness, prevention and response. OECD estimates suggest that ODA accounts for around 80% of financing for these activities in low-income countries, where the greatest risks often occur.
 The implications are particularly significant in countries facing disease outbreaks. The Democratic Republic of the Congo and Uganda are currently responding to Ebola outbreaks, while health ODA to both countries is projected to decline significantly.
 Read the policy brief: https://brnw.ch/21x3LBq
 #ODA2026 #OECDdevelopment

items.title: A job. A degree. A bachelor's pathway. All in one program. Chattanooga State, BlueCross BlueShield of Tennessee, and ETS...
items.content_text:
A job. A degree. A bachelor's pathway. All in one program.
 Chattanooga State, BlueCross BlueShield of Tennessee, and ETSU are creating even more opportunities for BlueHorizon apprentices.
 Students in BlueHorizon earn a Health Sciences degree from ChattState while working in paid positions at BlueCross BlueShield of Tennessee. Now, thanks to a new transfer agreement with ETSU, students can continue their education and earn a bachelor's degree in Professional Development.
 It's another way we're helping students gain real-world experience, earn a paycheck, and build a career with confidence.
 BlueHorizon will begin recruiting for its second cohort in August 2026, with classes starting in January 2027.

items.title: High school sports develops character and work ethic in student athletes and builds school pride for the entire student...
items.content_text:
High school sports develops character and work ethic in student athletes and builds school pride for the entire student body. The newest renderings of the new Brainerd Middle High School reflect our commitment to giving public school student athletes the tools and facilities to thrive. We released them at Brainerd Alumni events this weekend, which was a reminder of that the Panthers have one of the proudest alumni bases in our community.
 For too long, we underinvested in public school athletics, falling short of the expectations of students, parents, and coaches, and propelling private school athletic programs in the process. The school board is addressing this through increased coaching stipends, and so are we in county government with tens of millions being invested in new and improved public school athletic facilities. My goal is that when the next world class athlete like Ace Bailey comes along, he or she will have the confidence to follow in the tradition of generations of Brainerd Panthers who have succeeded in athletics, academics, and beyond their high school experience. 
 Brainerd High School

items.title: This morning we proposed a one year moratorium on data centers in Hamilton County. Frankly, I’m skeptical that they belo...
items.content_text:
This morning we proposed a one year moratorium on data centers in Hamilton County. Frankly, I’m skeptical that they belong in our rural communities, which are already seeing unprecedented residential development. This moratorium will give our new County Planning Department the opportunity to further study the impacts of data centers, engage our utility providers, and gather community feedback. 
 We have a responsibility to plan thoughtfully for the next generation and to preserve the beauty of our county.

items.content_text:
🎓✨ **Chattanooga Head Start Alumni Spotlight** ✨🎓 
 Chattanooga Head Start is proud to recognize Maurquez Thompson for his outstanding achievements and continued commitment to serving others.
 As the Co-Valedictorian of the Class of 2023 at Brainerd High School, Maurquez set an inspiring example of academic excellence, leadership, and determination. Today, as a rising senior at Stanford University, he continues to excel while remaining dedicated to giving back to the Chattanooga community that helped shape him.
 Maurquez is also a proud Chattanooga Head Start alumnus, and his journey is a powerful reminder of the lasting impact that Head Start can have on the lives of children and families. His success reflects what is possible when young children are provided with strong educational foundations, supportive relationships, and opportunities to thrive.
 We celebrate Maurquez not only for his academic accomplishments but also for his character, leadership, and commitment to uplifting others. He represents the promise, potential, and greatness that Head Start helps nurture every day.
 Congratulations, Maurquez! Your Chattanooga Head Start family is incredibly proud of you and look forward to seeing all that you will continue to accomplish.
 #ChattanoogaHeadStart #HeadStartWorks #HeadStartAlumni #FutureLeader #StanfordUniversity #BrainerdHighSchool #CommunityImpact #AcademicExcellence #GivingBack #ChattanoogaProud 🎉👏📚🌟

items.content_text:
The summer heat is here, and everyone is feeling its effects. To stay safe during extremely hot hours, it's important to recognize the signs of heat-related illnesses, such as cramps, dizziness, excessive sweating, and nausea.
 Certain groups are more vulnerable to the heat, including individuals working outdoors, older adults, pregnant women, young children, and those with chronic conditions. 
 To protect yourself, make sure to stay hydrated, wear lightweight clothing, and take breaks in the shade or air conditioning. Additionally, never leave children or pets in a parked car. 
 For more information on heat-related illnesses, please visit [CDC Heat Stress Information] https://www.cdc.gov/niosh/heat-stress/about/illnesses.html

items.title: Dangerous heat is expected across our area this week, with heat index values climbing well into the triple digits. High...
items.content_text:
Dangerous heat is expected across our area this week, with heat index values climbing well into the triple digits. High temperatures and humidity can quickly lead to heat-related illnesses.
 Stay hydrated by drinking plenty of water.
 Wear lightweight, light-colored clothing.
 Take frequent breaks in the shade or air conditioning.
 Never leave children or pets unattended in a vehicle—even for a moment.
 Check on elderly neighbors, family members, and anyone without reliable air conditioning.
 Signs of heat exhaustion include heavy sweating, weakness, dizziness, nausea, and headache. If someone becomes confused, loses consciousness, or stops sweating, call 911 immediately. These may be signs of life-threatening heat stroke.
 Hamilton County EMS encourages everyone to take this heat seriously. A few simple precautions can help keep you and your loved ones safe.

items.content_text:
Chattanooga! Outdoor temperatures may become dangerously high this week. Take extra care to stay hydrated. If it gets too hot, move indoors and don’t forget to bring your pets! 
 - Keep blinds and curtains closed to block out the sun
 - Use fans to help circulate air (set ceiling fans to spin counterclockwise)
 - Raise your thermostat a few degrees when you're not home
 - Skip the oven and dryer during peak heat hours
 - Check for air leaks around windows and doors
 Small changes can make a big impact!

items.title: ArtsBuild Launches Arts Voter Guide for Hamilton County Elections - Check it Out at the Link Below! CHATTANOOGA, TN
items.content_text:
ArtsBuild Launches Arts Voter Guide for Hamilton County Elections - Check it Out at the Link Below!
 CHATTANOOGA, TN – ArtsBuild has released its 2026 Arts Voter Guide, a nonpartisan resource highlighting where Hamilton County candidates stand on arts and culture. The guide features responses from candidates for Hamilton County Mayor, County Commission, and School Board.
 With early voting beginning July 17 and Election Day on August 6, ArtsBuild encourages voters to learn where candidates stand on issues affecting the arts. Hamilton County's nonprofit arts and culture sector employs more than 3,000 people and generates nearly $200 million in annual economic activity while enriching education, strengthening neighborhoods, and expanding access to creative opportunities.
 "The Arts Voter Guide is a resource for every Hamilton County voter," said ArtsBuild President James McKissic. "Our goal is to provide voters with the information they need to make informed decisions and better understand each candidate's perspective on the role of the arts in our community."
 As part of its mission to build stronger communities through the arts, ArtsBuild supports the nonprofit arts sector and advocates for arts education and access throughout Chattanooga and Hamilton County. The Arts Voter Guide is designed to help voters make informed decisions while remaining strictly nonpartisan.
 The guide is available now at artsbuild.com/advocacy.
 About ArtsBuild
 ArtsBuild is a Chattanooga-based 501(c)(3) nonprofit dedicated to building stronger communities through the arts. Since 1969, ArtsBuild has invested more than $80 million in Hamilton County through grants to arts organizations, arts education programs for students and teachers, and initiatives that expand access to the arts for all residents.  Support our work at: https://secure.qgiv.com/for/aaogc/

items.title: This week marks one year since Dr. Lori Bruce began her role as Chancellor of the University of Tennessee at Chattanooga...
items.content_text:
This week marks one year since Dr. Lori Bruce began her role as Chancellor of the University of Tennessee at Chattanooga.
